coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and test stripe-based assay of bacteria by using vancomycin-modified gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: You, Qi
Zhang, Xiaodong
Wu, Fu-Gen
Chen, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Bacteria are the most principal pathogens threatening human health. Rapid, simple, and sensitive identification and assay for bacteria are greatly desired for clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring. In this work, a commonly used antibiotic vancomycin (Van) was used to synthesize gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) by a simple one-pot reaction, and the resultant Van-coated Au NPs (denoted as Van-AuNPs) can selectively interact with Gram-positive bacteria and thus be used to differentiate Gram-positive bacteria from Gram-negative ones through the change of their red color. On this basis, Van-AuNPs-based visual colorimetric assay was established for Gram-positive bacteria. The detection limits of bare eyes for Gram-positive S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis are 1 × 109, 1 × 109, and 1 × 109 cells/mL, respectively, according to the color of the spots on the test strip without the use of any microscopic devices. We also used this method to detect S. aureus in orange juice and tap water with excellent recoveries. Compared with conventional bacterial identification methods, this assay is rapid and easy, and is promising to be used as a rapid method for the identification and quantification of bacteria in clinics, environment, and fieldwork.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacteria
Colorimetric assay
Vancomycin
Gold nanoparticles
S. aureus
M. luteus
B. subtilis
E. coli
P. vulgaris
P. aeruginosa

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Responses of Van-AuNPs to Gram-positive bacteria
Colorimetric assay of Gram-positive bacteria using Van-AuNPs
Colorimetric assay of S. aureus in orange juice and tap water using Van-AuNPs
Responses of Van-AuNPs to Gram-positive bacteria
Electron microscopy analysis of bacterial cells in the presence of Van-AuNPs
Colorimetric assay of S. aureus in orange juice and tap water using Van-AuNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Colorimetric assay of bacteria using vancomycin-decorated Au nanoparticles.
(A) TEM image and the corresponding size distribution (inset) of Van-AuNPs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Van-AuNP solution. Van-AuNPs were prepared with a Van-to-HAuCl4 molar ratio of 1:1.
FTIR spectra of Van and Van-AuNPs.
XPS curve of Au 4f in Van-AuNPs.

(A) Photographs of Van-AuNPs (a), Van-AuNPs + Gram-positive S. aureus (b), and Van-AuNPs + Gram-negative E. coli (c) after centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 5 min (Note: at this rate Van-AuNPs cannot be centrifuged). (B) Absorption spectra of Van-AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of S. aureus cells, respectively. (C) The liner relation of absorption intensity change ((A 0 − A)/A 0) versus the concentration of S. aureus. A 0 and A: the absorbance of supernatant at 519 nm in the absence and presence of S. aureus cells, respectively.

(A) Absorption spectra of Van-AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of E. coli. (B) The liner relation of absorption intensity change ((A 0 − A)/A 0) versus the concentration of E. coli. A 0 and A: the absorbance of supernatant at 519 nm in the absence and presence of E. coli cells, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra of Van-AuNPs in the presence of Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis, respectively. The concentration of the bacterial solution is 3 × 108 cells/mL, and water is used as a control.
TEM images of Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus cells (A) and Gram-negative bacteria E. coli cells (B) after incubation with Van-AuNPs.
Colorimetric detection of Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis, and Gram-negative bacteria E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and P. vulgaris. Blank is water.
Colorimetric detection of S. aureus in tap water (A) and orange juice (B).
Assay of S. aureus in tap water and orange juice using Van-AuNPs.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and test stripe-based assay of bacteria by using vancomycin-modified gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210096, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacteria are the most principal pathogens threatening human health. Rapid, simple, and sensitive identification and assay for bacteria are greatly desired for clinical diagnosis and environmental monitoring. In this work, a commonly used antibiotic vancomycin (Van) was used to synthesize gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) by a simple one-pot reaction, and the resultant Van-coated Au NPs (denoted as Van-AuNPs) can selectively interact with Gram-positive bacteria and thus be used to differentiate Gram-positive bacteria from Gram-negative ones through the change of their red color. On this basis, Van-AuNPs-based visual colorimetric assay was established for Gram-positive bacteria. The detection limits of bare eyes for Gram-positive S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis are 1 × 109, 1 × 109, and 1 × 109 cells/mL, respectively, according to the color of the spots on the test strip without the use of any microscopic devices. We also used this method to detect S. aureus in orange juice and tap water with excellent recoveries. Compared with conventional bacterial identification methods, this assay is rapid and easy, and is promising to be used as a rapid method for the identification and quantification of bacteria in clinics, environment, and fieldwork.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infection is a serious threat to human health. According to the report of the World Health Organization, >25% annual deaths (about 15 million) worldwide are estimated to be related to infectious diseases mainly caused by bacteria [1,2]. Bacterial infection can lead to sepsis, bacteremia, pneumonia, endocarditis, or even death [3]. Rapid, simple, and sensitive assays for bacteria are important and urgently needed for both clinical diagnosis and environmental test. Conventionally, bacterial cells are identified by plate count method or optical microscopy [4]. However, microscopic observation combined with various staining methods is usually time-consuming and laborious. Some non-microscopic methods are available for the identification of bacteria including immunoassays [5,6], nucleic acid-based methods [7,8], flow cytometry, mass spectrometry, and biochips [9–11]. Although these methods have been improved in sensitivity, automation, and convenience, they often fail to meet the clinical requirements for rapid identification of bacteria. For example, antigen–antibody-based immunoassays depend on the availability of high-quality antibodies. However, high-quality antibodies for unknown bacteria require complicated screenings by skilled technicians with specialized instruments, and the sensitivity of the assay is relatively low [4]. Nucleic acid amplification-based methods are highly sensitive. However, they require highly purified template/target DNA of unknown bacteria, which often have to be separated, enriched, and purified from the complex samples such as blood, urine, or sewage [7]. Flow cytometry and mass spectrometry require expensive devices. Therefore, quick, convenient, and sensitive assays for bacteria is highly desirable and challenging [10]. Recently, some rapid and convenient nanoparticle-based methods have been developed for the bacterial identification. Small molecules such as vancomycin-, folic acid-, amikacin-, and polyethylenimine-functionalized magnetic or metallic nanoparticles are easy to isolate from complex components and are used for the isolation and identification of bacteria [12–15].

Vancomycin (Van) is an antibiotic of the glycopeptide family, which can effectively interact (e.g., via hydrogen bonds) and inactivate a broad spectrum of Gram-positive bacteria by interfering with the synthesis of bacterial cell walls [16–18]. This specific action of Van has been used to prepare antibacterial materials [19–25]. Van-modified magnetic nanoparticles [26–28] and fluorescent carbon dots [29,30] have been used for the detection or capture of Gram-positive bacteria. Gold nanoparticle (AuNP)-based colorimetric assays have always been a highly sensitive and widely favored method because of their advantages of simplicity, quickness, and visualization [31,32]. However, to the best of our knowledge, AuNP-based visual colorimetric assay for bacteria has never been successfully developed.

Here, we report a simple, one-pot reaction approach to prepare Van-coated Au NPs (denoted as Van-AuNPs). The glycosides of Van molecules can directly reduce Au salts to form gold nanoparticles. The as-prepared Van-AuNPs can strongly interact with Gram-positive bacteria. In addition, with the help of silver enhancement, a highly sensitive visual colorimetric assay for Gram-positive bacteria was established (Scheme 1 ). This assay is rapid and easy, and does not require the use of any microscopic devices.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Vancomycin hydrochloride hydrate and nitrocellulose (NC) membranes were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The Silver Enhancer Kit was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. Tryptone and yeast extract were purchased from Oxford Co., Ltd. Lysogeny broth (LB) was prepared by dissolving 10 g of tryptone, 5 g of yeast extract, and 10 g of NaCl in 1 L of ultrapure water, sterilizing in 121 °C for 30 min, and adjusting its pH to 7.4 with NaOH solution. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions. Bacterial cells including Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Micrococcus luteus (M. luteus), and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli), Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) were purchased from the China Center of Industrial Culture Collection (Beijing, China).

The size and morphology of Van-AuNPs were observed by a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The absorption spectra were recorded using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded with a PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Ulvac-Phi). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific). Bacterial cells were counted by a flow cytometer (NovoCyte 2060R, ACEA).

Van-AuNPs were synthesized through a one-pot reaction between HAuCl4 and Van. Typically, 500 μL of Van aqueous solution (1 mM) and 500 μL of HAuCl4 aqueous solution (1 mM) were mixed. They were stirred in a reaction vial for 2 min followed by the addition of 10 μL of NaOH (10 M). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h in the dark. The generated Van-AuNPs were washed with water for 3 times by centrifugation (14,000 rpm). The Van-AuNPs were finally dispersed in 2 mL of water and stored at 4 °C for further use. The concentration of the Van-AuNP solution was determined to be approximately 6 nM by UV–vis measurement according to the Beer’s law (ε: 2.0 × 108 M–1 cm–1 at 520 nm) [33,34].

The binding amount of Van on the Van-AuNPs was estimated by the following equation: Binding amount of Van = total amount of Van drug fed initially – amount of unbinding Van. The unbinding Van was obtained by centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for 1 h, and quantified by UV–vis spectroscopy using the absorption peak at 281 nm of Van.

Six species of bacterial cells, including three species of Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis) and three species of Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, P. vulgaris, and P. aeruginosa), were chosen as test samples. These bacterial cells were propagated through incubating with 15 mL of LB medium in an incubator at 37 °C for 12 h. Then, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 5 min. After washed twice with water, the bacterial cells were dispersed in water, counted by a flow cytometer, and diluted to different concentrations with water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of Van-AuNPs to Gram-positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of bacterial cell solutions (500 μL) were added into 500 μL of Van-AuNP solutions (6 nM) to obtain the final bacterial cell concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0, and 8.0 × 108 cells/mL, respectively. These mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. UV–vis absorption intensities at 519 nm of the supernatants were measured. The precipitates were collected for TEM characterization after washing twice with water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of Gram-positive bacteria using Van-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: For Van-AuNP-based colorimetric assay, 500 μL of Van-AuNP solutions and 500 μL of different concentrations of bacterial cell solutions were mixed to obtain the final bacterial concentrations of 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, and 8 × 1010 cells/mL, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The mixtures were centrifugated at 7000 rpm for 5 min, and the precipitates (containing bacterial cells and Van-AuNPs) were washed twice with water via centrifugation, and resuspended in water. Then, 4 μL of the obtained solutions were dropped onto a nitrocellulose membrane strip. After a few minutes of drying, the red spots on the strip were photographed. For the following silver enhancement procedure, 8 μL of silver enhancement solution was added onto the spots of the strip. After several minutes, the strip was rinsed with water, and fixed with 2.5% sodium thiosulfate aqueous solution (4 μL per spot). Finally, the black spots on the strip were photographed and the degree of black was expressed in gray value by Adobe Photoshop software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of S. aureus in orange juice and tap water using Van-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Orange juice (Fanta) was purchased from a local supermarket. Tap water is from a local water supply company. The samples of tap water and orange juice containing different amounts of S. aureus cells were prepared by adding a known number of S. aureus cells to tap water and orange juice, respectively. After S. aureus cells were washed for three times with water, 500 μL of Van-AuNP solutions and different concentrations of bacterial cell solutions (500 μL) were mixed to obtain the final bacterial concentrations of 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 × 1010 cells/mL, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The following colorimetric assay was similar to the procedures mentioned in Section 2.6.

A facile one-pot reaction of HAuCl4 with Van in an alkaline solution of pH 14 was conducted. The generated solution is red in color and has a maximal absorption peak at 519 nm (Fig. 1 B). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the as-prepared Van-AuNPs are spherical, and the average size is approximately 9.5 ± 1.5 nm (Fig. 1A).

The different molar ratios of HAuCl4 to Van have an effect on the size of Au NPs (Fig. S1 and S2). A Van-to-HAuCl4 molar ratio of 1:1 produced the most stable Van-AuNPs (which could be stably stored for at least 3 months at 4 °C, Fig. S3), which were used for the following experiments. We also found that without Van, no AuNPs were formed, indicating that Van acted as the reducing and stabilizing agent. Furthermore, we estimated the binding amount of Van on the Van-AuNPs to be 3.85 nmol/mg (Fig. S4).

In addition, FTIR was conducted to verify the binding of Van with AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 2 , the as-prepared Van-AuNPs displayed the characteristic bands of Van at 3336, 1660, and 1232/1060 cm−1, which are assigned to the stretching vibration of NH/OH, CO, and CO of Van, respectively. Compared with Van, Van-AuNPs also show the decreased band intensities of the CO stretching vibrations at 1232 and 1060 cm−1, suggesting that the glycoside of Van containing the CO bond might be involved in the binding with AuNPs. To understand the valence states of Au in the prepared AuNPs, we conducted XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 , the Au 4f7/2 peak of Van-AuNPs could be deconvoluted into two components centered at 82.2 and 83.9 eV, which can be assigned to Au(0) and Au(I), respectively [35,36]. The results indicate that HAuCl4 was reduced to Au(0) and Au(I) by Van. In addition, the XPS peaks of O, N, and C elements (Fig. S5) further validate the presence of Van on the surface of AuNPs because only Van contains these elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of Van-AuNPs to Gram-positive bacteria
Electron microscopy analysis of bacterial cells in the presence of Van-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated the microstructures of bacterial cells incubated with Van-AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 7 , TEM images reveal that Van-AuNPs were apparently anchored on the surface of Gram-positive S. aureus cell walls whereas almost no Van-AuNPs can be observed on the surface of Gram-negative E. coli cell walls after careful washing of these cells. The results confirm that the Van-AuNPs can specifically interact with Gram-positive bacteria like S. aureus. It has been reported that the antibacterial activity of Van is attributed to its interference on the synthesis of Gram-positive bacterial cell wall by its specific binding to the terminal d-alanyl-d-alanine of peptidoglycan [17,18]. Our TEM results intuitively confirm this conclusion.

We next investigated the color change of Gram-positive bacterial cell suspensions mixed with Van-AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 8 , when different concentrations of Gram-positive bacterial cells were dropped onto a nitrocellulose membrane strip for colorimetric assay, the red color of Gram-positive bacterial cells (S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis) became deepened with increasing bacterial concentrations, whereas the white color of Gram-negative bacterial cells (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and P. vulgaris) only had slight changes at low bacterial concentrations. Silver enhancement is a method of enhancing the color signal of Au NPs by a color change from red to black resulting from the reduction of silver ions on the surface of Au NPs. According to the color change of Van-AuNPs and the color change using the Ag enhancement approach, the detection limits (based on bare eyes) were ∼2 × 109 and ∼1 × 109 cells/mL, respectively, for S. aureus (Fig. 8A), ∼1 × 109 cells/mL and ∼1 × 109 cells/mL, respectively, for M. luteus (Fig. 8B), and ∼1 × 109 cells/mL and ∼1 × 109 cells/mL, respectively, for B. subtilis (Fig. 8C). For per spot assay, the detection limits of S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis are 4 × 106, 4 × 106, and 4 × 106 cells, respectively, based on the addition of 4 μL of bacterial suspension onto each spot. The reason nitrocellulose membranes were selected as the holder is that they can efficiently adsorb bacterial cells (possibly via peptidoglycans) and the Au/Ag materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of S. aureus in orange juice and tap water using Van-AuNPs

ce:section.ce:para: A facile one-pot preparation method for Van-AuNPs has been successfully developed through a direct reduction of HAuCl4 by vancomycin. The as-prepared Van-AuNPs can specifically interact with Gram-positive bacteria, which can be used not only to discriminate Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria but also to quantify Gram-positive bacteria by a colorimetric assay on nitrocellulose membrane strips. The detection limits based on bare eyes are 1 × 109, 1 × 109, and 1 × 109 cells/mL for Gram-positive S. aureus, M. luteus, and B. subtilis, respectively without the use of any microscopic devices. This method for the determination of S. aureus in real orange juice and tap water displays excellent detection recoveries. Compared with conventional bacterial identification methods, this bacterial assay is simple, fast, and easy to operate, and is promising to be used as a rapid method for the identification and quantification of bacteria in clinics, environment, and fieldwork.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant No. 21575023), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20161414), and the Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. KYLX0190).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.103.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The perpetual challenge of infectious diseases
The challenge of emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases
Filtration capture immunoassay for bacteria: optimization and potential for urinalysis
Development of the sensitive lateral flow immunoassay with silver enhancement for the detection of Ralstonia Solanacearum in potato tubers
Rapid methods for the detection of foodborne bacterial pathogens: principles, applications, advantages and limitations
A quantitative PCR-ELISA for the rapid enumeration of bacteria in refrigerated raw milk
Rapid detection of respiring Escherichia coli O157: H7 in apple juice, milk, and ground beef by flow cytometry
Bacterial identification: from the agar plate to the mass spectrometer
Dual recognition strategy for specific and sensitive detection of bacteria using aptamer-coated magnetic beads and antibiotic-capped gold nanoclusters
Solid-state synthesis of self-functional carbon quantum dots for detection of bacteria and tumor cells
One-step synthesis of amikacin modified fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of Gram-negative bacteria like Escherichia coli
A rapid SERS method for label-free bacteria detection using polyethylenimine-modified Au-coated magnetic microspheres and Au@Ag nanoparticles
The vancomycin group of antibiotics and the fight against resistant bacteria
Quaternized silicon nanoparticles with polarity-sensitive fluorescence for selectively imaging and killing Gram-positive bacteria
Multifunctional Fe3O4@Au nanoeggs as photothermal agents for selective killing of nosocomial and antibiotic-resistant bacteria
Vancomycin-hybrid bimetallic Au/Ag composite nanoparticles: preparation of the nanoparticles and characterization of the antibacterial activity
Vancomycin-modified mesoporous silica nanoparticles for selective recognition and killing of pathogenic Gram-positive bacteria over macrophage-like cells
Potent antibacterial nanoparticles for pathogenic bacteria
Vancomycin-modified Fe3O4@SiO2@Ag microflowers as effective antimicrobial agents
Ubiquitous detection of Gram-positive bacteria with bioorthogonal magnetofluorescent nanoparticles
Using biofunctional magnetic nanoparticles to capture vancomycin-resistant enterococci and other Gram-positive bacteria at ultralow concentration
Affinity capture using vancomycin-bound magnetic nanoparticles for the MALDI-MS analysis of bacteria
Employing carbon dots modified with vancomycin for assaying Gram-positive bacteria like Staphylococcus aureus
Carbon dot-based platform for simultaneous bacterial distinguishment and antibacterial applications
Scanometric DNA array detection with nanoparticle probes
Gold nanoparticles in chemical and biological sensing
Gold and alloy nanoparticles in solution and thin film assembly: spectrophotometric determination of molar absorptivity
Spectral properties and relaxation dynamics of surface plasmon electronic oscillations in gold and silver nanodots and nanorods
Protein-directed synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Pressure-induced fluorescence enhancement of the BSA-protected gold nanoclusters and the corresponding conformational changes of protein

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qi You is a PhD candidate at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His current research interest focuses on bacterial analysis.
Xiaodong Zhang is a PhD candidate at Southeast University (Nanjing, P. R. China). His current research interest focuses on fluorescent nanomaterials.
Fu-Gen Wu is a professor at Southeast University. His major research interest focuses on cell imaging and biomaterials.
Yang Chen is a professor at the State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics of Southeast University. His major research interest focuses on bioanalytical chemistry and luminescent biosensors.
